title: Tennis Player Says Line-Calling System Was Wrong
summary: Elena Rybakina, a tennis player from Kazakhstan, played a match in Madrid, Spain. She was not happy because the machine that calls if a ball is in or out made a mistake. The machine said a serve by her opponent, Zheng Qinwen from China, was good, but Rybakina thought it was out. She asked the umpire to check the mark on the clay court, but the umpire said she could not. Rybakina said she does not trust the machine anymore. Last year, another player named Alexander Zverev also had a problem with the same machine. He took a picture of a ball mark and got a warning. Rybakina said the mark was right in front of the umpire, so it was frustrating. She still won the match 4-6, 6-4, 6-3.

when a player hits the ball to start a point

feeling upset because you cannot do what you want

background_read: In tennis, there are lines on the court to show where the ball must land. Sometimes, a machine uses cameras to check if the ball is in or out. This is called electronic line-calling. Players can ask the umpire to check a mark on clay courts, but the umpire might not always do that. This can make players upset if they think the machine made a mistake.
Article_Structure: WHO: Elena Rybakina, a tennis player from Kazakhstan, and Zheng Qinwen from China.
WHAT: Rybakina was upset because the electronic line-calling system made a mistake on a serve.
WHERE: At the Madrid Open tennis tournament in Madrid, Spain.
WHY: Rybakina thought the ball was out but the system called it in, and the umpire would not check the mark.

perspectives.description: Rybakina thinks the system is wrong and she cannot trust it. She believes the umpire should have checked the mark.
The umpire said she could not check the mark because the electronic system is used to make the decision.
